summary: Camille Fournier argues that while 'Choose Boring Technology' has become accepted wisdom, engineering leaders need to extend this principle to planning itself. She observes that Platform Engineering teams especially suffer when management relies on vision alone rather than concrete, predictable plans. Novel technology initiatives require boring, incremental planning to succeed—starting with proof of concepts, documenting learnings, and communicating clear roadmaps. Good strategy isn't about razzle-dazzle innovation but about identifying core problems so solutions become obvious. Leaders who fail to make boring plans create unnecessary stress and anxiety for their teams, while those who embrace predictable planning free their organizations from the tyranny of uncertainty.
key_points: Vision alone is insufficient—'hire smart people and get out of their way' breaks down for platform teams where downstream dependencies create high stakes
Platform teams often absorb innovation tokens forced upon them by other teams building shadow infrastructure, then inherit operational expectations without proper groundwork
Boring plans mean starting with proof of concepts, documenting learnings iteratively, and communicating predictable roadmaps to both team and users
Good strategy makes solutions obvious and simple; strategies relying on 'unproven magic bullets' are bad strategies
Constantly changing plans signal either false certainty or insufficient research—both waste time and stress teams
money_quotes: It is a common belief in engineering that, with a clear enough vision, the rest of the pieces of work will fall into place.
Strategy is not in the business of razzle-dazzle, it's in the business of getting to the core of the issues so that the solution becomes simple and obvious.
Plans that are formed around hope are failing plans; hope is not a plan.
Plans that change constantly are failing plans.
When your plans are constantly changing, it is a sign that you either are making plans that express a certainty you don't have, or you haven't done your research to get the right certainty in place.
You owe it your teams, and to your users, to free them from the tyranny and stress of uncertainty.
key_insight: When working with novel or high-stakes technology, leaders must compensate by making their plans predictable, incremental, and boring—because hope is not a plan and constant change signals insufficient preparation.
